turns.content: I FOUND MATSUTAKE. actual matsutake. in Quebec. I'm still shaking
Seriously? Matsutake in Quebec? That's a rare find! Where were you foraging?

up near Mont-Tremblant, there's a spot I go with this guy Jean-Paul who's been foraging for like thirty years. he knows every trail, every microclimate. we were looking for chanterelles and hen-of-the-woods, which we found plenty of, and then I smelled something. this sharp, almost cinnamony pine smell, and I looked down and there they were. three perfect matsutake just sitting there under a red pine
Three of them! The fact that you identified them by smell first says a lot about your instincts. Did Jean-Paul confirm what you'd found?

he came over and his face — putain — he looked like he'd seen the Virgin Mary. he said in thirty years he's found matsutake maybe five times in Quebec. they're mostly a Pacific Northwest and Japanese thing. finding them here is like... I don't know, finding truffles in your backyard. he made me take a photo of the exact spot and the GPS coordinates so we can come back next year
That's the kind of foraging moment people talk about for years. So what's the plan for them? Three matsutake in the hands of a professional chef is a lot of potential.

ok so this is where I might sound insane. I brought them home and I just... looked at them. for like an hour. I held them and smelled them and turned them over in my hands. they're so beautiful. the texture of the cap, the way the gills are still tight and closed — these are button stage, which is the most prized. I almost don't want to cook them
That's not insane at all — you found something genuinely rare and you're savouring the moment before it becomes food. But eventually... what are you thinking?

the Japanese do it best. dobin mushi — matsutake in a clear dashi broth, steamed in a teapot. the mushroom flavour is so delicate that you need to let it be the star. no butter, no cream, no heavy sauces. just the mushroom and the broth and maybe a sliver of yuzu. c'est tout. simplicity is the hardest thing in cooking
Dobin mushi would be a beautiful way to honour the find — letting the matsutake speak without anything competing. Are you going to cook this at home or try to work it into the restaurant somehow?

home. these are mine. I found them with my own hands in the forest and they're not going on any menu. I'm going to make the dobin mushi tonight, alone, in my kitchen in Villeray, and I'm going to sit there and eat it slowly and probably cry a little because I'm pathetic like that. Babushka always said the best meals are the ones you earn with your hands. picking mushrooms in the forest is exactly what she meant

That sounds like a perfect evening, honestly. Some ingredients deserve to be a private experience rather than a menu item. Your Babushka would approve of eating what you found with your own hands — that's the most honest kind of cooking there is. Enjoy every sip of that broth.

merci. I'm going to start the dashi now. I also brought home about two kilos of chanterelles and a beautiful hen-of-the-woods that weighs almost a kilo by itself. those I'll bring to work — the chanterelles will go into a velouté and the hen-of-the-woods I want to roast whole and serve with brown butter and hazelnuts. but the matsutake — those are sacred. ok I'm going to stop talking about mushrooms before you think I've completely lost it. bonne soirée
You haven't lost it — finding matsutake in Quebec is genuinely exciting. Go make that dobin mushi. And if you cry a little, that just means it's good. Bonne soirée, Nadia.
